🚨Upozorňujeme na zranitelnost v Linux kernelu, CVE-2026-43499. Zranitelnost s označením GhostLock je typu use-after-free v implementaci real-time mutexů (CONFIG_RT_MUTEXES), je přítomná v jádře od roku 2011 a zasahuje široké spektrum verzí (od v2.6.39-rc1 až po v7.1-rc1), přičemž umožňuje lokálně přihlášenému uživateli bez zvláštních oprávnění eskalovat privilegia na úroveň root. Podstata chyby spočívá v tom, že při chybném návratu z operace zámku dojde k nekorektnímu „úklidu“ čekajících struktur, kdy jádro pracuje se stavem nesprávné úlohy a ponechá si odkaz na již uvolněnou paměť. Tento zastaralý odkaz může být následně znovu využit v jiném kontextu, což útočníkovi umožní manipulovat s během jádra a převzít kontrolu nad systémem. Exploit je veřejně dostupný, má vysokou úspěšnost a dokáže obejít i kontejnerovou izolaci. Oprava spočívá ve změně logiky práce s waiter strukturami pro správnou manipulaci se stavem úloh.   📌Doporučujeme aplikovat upstream commit 3bfdc63936dd nebo aktualizovat na opravené verze distribucí, například Debian (bullseye 6.1.176-1~deb11u1, bookworm 6.1.176-1, trixie 6.12.95-1, forky 7.0.13-1, sid 7.1.3-1), Ubuntu resolute (7.0.0-27.27) a další.

VMRay June 2026 detection roundup ships an AdaptixC2 config extractor, 5 new VTIs mapped to MITRE ATT&CK, and 30+ YARA rules covering stealers, RATs, ransomware, and phishing lures.  - AdaptixC2, the open-source post-exploitation framework increasingly abused by threat actors alongside legitimate red teams, now has a config extractor in VMRay. Pulling C2 config at detonation time surfaces listener type and encryption settings without manual reverse engineering.  - Five new or improved VTIs add behavioral coverage: DNS domain discovery (T1016) flags sandbox-evasion and target-validation logic; system time queries (T1124) surface delay and time-zone checks; a meta-VTI for malicious documents fires when several macro-behavior signals combine, catching cases where the full payload chain never completes; email Reply-To spoofing with recently registered domains (T1684/002) and Microsoft device enrollment link abuse round out the set.  - 30+ YARA rules ship across Discord-based stealers (GurcuStealer, OiboiStealer, RIVA-S, TikiStelaer, WeedHack), RATs (LimeRAT, PulsarRAT, SeroRAT, Overlord, PackClientRAT), ransomware (XiaoBa, DemonXLocker, Jaku), loaders (UpCrypter, BRDownloader), Covenant and PhonyC2 backdoors, SimpleHelp RMM, and phishing content including DocuSign lures, ClickFix/pastejacking, and fake CAPTCHAs.  Full YARA rules and VTI details are in the VMRay report.  #DFIR_Radar

Public AI endpoints are a massive telemetry leak when you are running target profile scrapes or malware analysis. Every CVE search or payload lookup you send out exposes your operation.  To fix this, we containerized SearXNG and LiteLLM inside an LXC container on Proxmox (NextGenPVE).  Our target research loop now runs completely locally:  Operator requests target profile investigation. Custom agent queries SearXNG (privacy-respecting metasearch) to scrape search results anonymously. Text contents are cleaned and parsed. Local abliterated LLM extracts IoCs and maps TTPs to MITRE ATT&CK. No queries, target domains, or malware hashes ever leave our network boundaries.  Full architecture writeup and configurations: nextgenredteam.com/blog/buil…

Pentesting is mostly guesswork with a report attached. Built AuditOps Security to change that. Structured red/purple team engagements with a fully documented framework: 8 components from ROE to cleanup, mapped to MITRE ATT&CK. You get findings AND auditable evidence.

Threat actors like UNC6692 are evolving their social engineering tactics. Instead of relying solely on email phishing, they are chaining communication channels to weaponize the enterprise tools we trust every day.   By manufacturing an operational crisis (like an email-bombing campaign) and stepping in with a fake solution via Teams, adversaries are easily bypassing traditional email security perimeters.  The ultimate payload?   The SNOW Malware Ecosystem: a pipeline designed to quietly tunnel through networks, dump credentials, and compromise domain controllers.  Security teams need to understand how these multi-stage campaigns operate across endpoints, networks, and identity infrastructure to stop them before lateral movement occurs.  We just published a comprehensive breakdown of the UNC6692 campaign to help you defend against these tactics, including: 🔹 A breakdown on how the SNOW toolkit works 🔹 A deep dive into the legitimate cloud services and forensic tools used extract Active Directory data 🔹 The full MITRE ATT&CK mappings to help your SOC detect these behaviors early  Read it on the blog: xtra.li/4aMpcnT

We built our SIEM capability around getting past the noise you hit at Step 7 - alert triage. Instead of just dumping logs, LynxTrac links alerts to specific MITRE ATT&CK tactics and automatically tracks file and registry changes tied to suspicious activity. It's not just about seeing alerts but understanding which ones actually show something new or unusual in context.  One thing that trips up a lot of SOC analysts early on is sifting through alerts without a clear baseline. Our registry and file integrity monitoring lets you flag changes on-demand to reset what "normal" looks like before alerts flood in. That means less guesswork about whether an alert is a noise or a real threat.  How are you or your team handling false positives during triage? Do you have a way to quickly tie alerts back to system changes or threat frameworks, or is it mostly manual for now?

Most security teams have never tested whether their defences actually work.  Not in theory. Not against the threat actors that are actively targeting their industry.  Group-IB's Purple Teaming service changes that.  Unlike traditional penetration testing, Purple Teaming is a live, collaborative exercise. Our red team executes adversary scenarios while your defenders monitor, respond, and tune detection rules in real time. The gap between what your tools are supposed to do and what they actually do gets closed within a single engagement.  Every scenario is grounded in Group-IB's Threat Intelligence and mapped to MITRE ATT&CK® — drawn from 1,600+ high-tech cybercrime investigations conducted since 2003. That means your team isn't practising against a generic framework. They're training against the specific tactics, techniques, and procedures of threat actors targeting organisations like yours.  Exercises cover ransomware simulations, Active Directory attacks, supply chain compromise, and data exfiltration, delivered on-site, remotely, or hybrid, over one to eight weeks.  The result: measurably improved detection coverage, updated response playbooks, and a security team that has performed under pressure.  Purple Teaming is available now, globally, through Group-IB's Digital Crime Resistance Centers across Asia-Pacific, Europe, the Middle East and Africa, the Americas, and Central Asia.  🔗 Read More: link.group-ib.com/44mwafP

First-Ever 1- Click Android 17 Exploit Allows Attackers to Gain Full Control Over Your Android Phone  Source: cybersecuritynews.com/androi…  A full-chain exploit dubbed "IonStack" demonstrates how a single malicious URL click can hand attackers complete control over an Android device.  The proof-of-concept, described as the world's first public Android 17 root demo, chains two zero-day vulnerabilities spanning Firefox and the Linux kernel to achieve remote code execution and privilege escalation without further user interaction.  The exploit chain works by first compromising the Firefox renderer process through the browser flaw, then pivoting to the underlying Linux kernel (which powers Android) to break out of sandbox restrictions entirely.  #cybersecuritynews #android

sample_tweets.text: like lazarus group?

Lazarus Group has been stealing cryptocurrency since at least 2017. Billions of dollars worth. The money goes to the weapons programs. That part is not new, and by now it should surprise exactly nobody.  What Cyfirma's July 10, 2026 weekly intelligence report adds to that picture is the operational detail, and some of it is worth sitting with for a moment.  The specific technical shift documented in the report is the move toward direct IP-based command-and-control communications. Famous Chollima, which is how CrowdStrike and others track the same cluster DPRK-watchers call Lazarus Group, has been pulling away from domain-based C2 infrastructure. The reason is not complicated: the threat intelligence sharing ecosystem has gotten good enough at burning domains that domain-based indicators of compromise have a shorter operational shelf life than they used to. If your domains keep showing up in ISAC feeds and getting blocked within days, you route around the problem. Direct IP comms are harder to detect with the same signature-matching approaches, and the indicators don't circulate the same way. It's a straightforward adaptation. The kind of thing you do when you've watched your own operations get documented and you adjust accordingly.  The DLL injection piece is worth explaining plainly. When malicious code executes inside the memory space of a legitimate process, say a system utility or a security tool that the endpoint protection software trusts, the endpoint detection system sees the legitimate process running and may not flag the malicious thread inside it. It is not a new technique. What matters is that Famous Chollima is deploying it at scale as part of a botnet architecture that Cyfirma describes as a core operational pillar, not just a tool for one campaign.  That botnet infrastructure is the part of this assessment that tends to get underweighted in the coverage. The emphasis usually falls on individual heists or specific malware samples. But the persistent botnet access is what makes the rest of it possible. Compromised machines sitting dormant in that infrastructure can be activated for cryptocurrency theft, for ransomware, for espionage exfiltration, for destructive wiper deployment. The same access serves different mission types depending on what Pyongyang needs from a given operation at a given moment. That kind of flexible, persistent infrastructure takes time and resources to build and maintain. The fact that North Korea has been investing in it steadily since at least 2017 reflects a deliberate strategic choice to treat cyber operations as a combined revenue-generation and intelligence-collection program, with the revenue side funding the rest of the state apparatus including, per multiple intelligence assessments, the nuclear and ballistic missile programs.  The aggregate number that gets cited most often is somewhere north of three billion dollars stolen through cyber operations since 2017. UN Panel of Experts reports, CISA advisories, the 2022 Axie Infinity Ronin Bridge hack alone at around 620 million dollars. These are not rounding errors. This is a sanctions-evading revenue stream that has proven durable and difficult to interdict at scale, partly because the laundering infrastructure has also matured, with Tornado Cash and various mixers and OTC brokers in jurisdictions that don't cooperate with US law enforcement playing roles that have been documented repeatedly.  Cyfirma's report situates the Famous Chollima activity within a broader July 2026 threat landscape that includes simultaneous campaigns from Chinese, Russian, and Iranian state-sponsored actors across financial sector, critical infrastructure, and government network targets. The framing around the 2026 US midterms is in the report, and it is worth being clear about what that does and doesn't mean. The convergence of multiple state-sponsored campaigns in an election period is real, but it is also somewhat reflexive to frame every intelligence reporting cycle near an election as election-related. Chinese actors targeting critical infrastructure and financial networks are doing things they do continuously. Russian actors running influence operations are doing things they do continuously. The election framing captures something about the compounded defensive burden, the way that multiple simultaneous campaigns strain attribution resources and response capacity, but it can also flatten the distinct operational logics of actors who are pursuing different objectives that have nothing to do with each other or with the election.  What the Cyfirma assessment is more specifically documenting in the North Korea case is the maturation arc. The 2014 Sony Pictures attack was destructive and loud and not particularly sophisticated in retrospect. The WannaCry attribution in 2017, despite the initial chaos around understanding what it was, pointed to an actor that was still using relatively blunt instruments. What we are looking at now, per this and multiple other 2026 assessments, is an actor that has spent roughly a decade iterating on capability development, learning from documented failures, adjusting tradecraft in response to public reporting, and building infrastructure that supports sustained access rather than single destructive strikes.  The shift from loud destructive attacks to quiet persistent presence is, if you have been watching this space for a while, the more concerning trajectory. A wiper attack causes damage you can see and respond to. A botnet that sits dormant in financial sector infrastructure, activated periodically for exfiltration or pre-positioned for future activation, is harder to detect, harder to attribute in real time, and harder to explain to executives who want to understand the risk they're carrying.  The direct IP C2 adaptation specifically signals something about how Famous Chollima processes its own operational history. The public reporting on DPRK campaigns has been extensive. CISA, NSA, FBI, the UK NCSC, South Korean intelligence, private sector threat research firms, all of them have published detailed technical indicators. Lazarus Group has had a front-row seat to watching its own tooling get documented and burned. The response, as reflected in this July 2026 Cyfirma report, is not to stop operating. It is to keep operating while adjusting the specific techniques that have been most effectively countered.  That is what an adaptive threat actor looks like in practice. Not a static toolkit. An organization that reads the public reporting about itself and updates accordingly.  The financial motivation also creates a different incentive structure than pure espionage operations. A Chinese APT stealing intellectual property has operational security incentives that push toward patience and stealth. Famous Chollima is sometimes under pressure to generate revenue, which means some operations carry a different risk tolerance. The Ronin Bridge hack was not subtle. But the botnet infrastructure described in the Cyfirma report is the patient, persistent side of the house. These are not in contradiction. They are different mission types served by the same underlying capability investment.  The broader multi-vector framing in the report reflects something genuine about the current environment. Defensive resources are finite. When attribution work and incident response capacity is being consumed by overlapping campaigns from four different state-sponsored programs simultaneously, the actors who are willing to be patient benefit. Attribution gets muddier. Response prioritization forces tradeoffs. The actors who have been doing this long enough have probably figured out that operating in a crowded threat environment has its own advantages.  None of this is especially comforting, and none of it is especially surprising if you've been paying attention. The Cyfirma report is useful primarily as a current-state documentation of where Famous Chollima's tradecraft sits in mid-2026, and the answer is: more capable, more adaptive, and more structurally embedded in North Korea's sanctions-evasion economy than it was five years ago.  foreigninterference.org/post… #foreigninterference #AdvancedPersistentThreatOperations #MalwareDistribution #CriminalBotnetWeaponization #CryptocurrencyTheft #CyberEspionage

A 17-year-old in the Philippines was earning more from a mobile game than his parents earned from their jobs.  During a global pandemic. With no office. No boss. No degree.  Just a smartphone and three digital pets.  This is the story of Axie Infinity, and the moment Web3 gaming changed the world  The year is 2021. The Philippines was under strict COVID lockdowns.  Millions are out of work.  Families are struggling.  Then something strange starts spreading through communities:  People are making real money playing a game called @AxieInfinity Axie Infinity.  Not streaming it. Not selling merchandise. Just playing it..  Axie Infinity is a game where players collect, breed, and battle digital creatures called Axies.  Each Axie is an NFT,  a unique digital asset owned by the player.  When you win battles, you earn tokens (SLP) that have real monetary value.  You could play for a few hours a day and earn more than the minimum wage.  In the Philippines. In 2021. During a pandemic.  The numbers were staggering.  At its peak, Axie Infinity had 2.7 million daily active players.  Was generating $15 million in daily revenue.  Had a total economy worth over $9 billion.  Entire villages in the Philippines shifted to playing Axie as their primary income.  Scholars players who borrowed Axies from investors to play  were splitting earnings 70/30.  A new economy had appeared from nowhere.  Then in March 2022, the whole thing nearly collapsed overnight.  Hackers later identified as North Korea's Lazarus Group  stole $625 million from Axie's network.  The largest crypto hack in history at the time.  The token price crashed  The economy crumbled  Families who had built their livelihoods around the game watched their income evaporate in days.  But here's what Axie Infinity proved even in collapse:  Play-to-earn works as a concept.  Real people earned real money from a digital game during the hardest economic period of their lives.  The failure wasn't the idea. It was the economic design too dependent on new players joining to sustain rewards for existing ones.  A lesson every Web3 game developer is studying intensely right now.  The next generation of Web3 games is being built with these lessons baked in.  And with Better tokenomics,  Sustainable reward systems,  Games that are actually fun first  and profitable  Because a game people play to earn but don't enjoy playing is just a job.  And Web3 gaming's real promise is something much bigger than a job.  It's ownership of your items.   Your characters, Your progress.  In a game world that can't take it away from you.  Follow for Web3 stories that go beyond the headlines.  Raji

Brendan Foody started Mercor when he was 19 years old. Now he’s 23, worth billions on paper, and running one of the fastest-growing companies in AI history. On Thursday, his $10 billion business acquired startup Deeptune, a company Foody quietly helped fund months before buying it, Fortune learned exclusively.

Deeptune, an Andreessen Horowitz-backed startup, builds simulation environments where AI agents practice real-world tasks before touching production systems—think a flight simulator, but for AI agents learning how to use Excel, Salesforce, and Slack. Financial terms weren’t disclosed. And according to Mercor, Deeptune’s team will be relocating to New York.

Foody was listed as an angel investor in Deeptune’s $43 million Series A just three months before the acquisition closed. Foody told me the angel check was written with acquisition already in mind: “It was in a lot of ways the main motivation, actually.” Call it relationship-building or call it a strategic hold. Either way, a16z funded Foody’s future acquisition target, and Foody got a front-row seat before making his move.

The deal is also a direct answer to where the AI training market is heading. Frontier labs (and Mercor customers) like Anthropic and OpenAI now need full digital replicas of enterprise software environments where their agents can practice, fail, and learn. Mercor’s network of more than five million domain experts already builds the tasks and scoring rubrics that tell a model whether it did the job right. Deeptune builds the apps those tasks run inside. Together, they cover the full stack.

Foody claims Mercor is already the market leader in building these so-called reinforcement learning environments—and that all of the Mag Seven except Tesla are customers. The company hit $2 billion in ARR in June, up from $1 billion last year. Foody described the jump as “the fastest growth trajectory ever” from $1 million to $2 billion ARR in 24 months.

Mercor’s acquisition comes on the heels of a massive data breach in March. Hackers exploited a supply chain vulnerability in LiteLLM—an open-source Python library widely used to build AI tools—and exfiltrated an estimated four terabytes of Mercor’s data. This included contractor Social Security numbers, passport scans, interview recordings, facial biometrics, and internal records revealing how Anthropic labels safety data and how Meta builds reinforcement learning pipelines. The hacking group Lapsus$ claimed responsibility and offered the data for sale on Telegram. A class action was filed in April against Mercor in California. 

Foody spoke about the breach like a thing of the past, with the confidence of someone whose customers already voted with their wallets: “Every frontier lab has expanded their relationship with us since the data breach,” he claimed. “The business grew from a $1 billion revenue run rate to $2 billion in ARR in the last four months.”

That’s either a remarkable display of customer loyalty or a reminder that Frontier Labs has nowhere else to go at this scale. 

See you tomorrow,

Lily Mae Lazarus

Major crypto exchange AscendEX has abruptly shut down operations as of July 1, leaving users unable to withdraw funds and the platform unresponsive to claims.  Once known as BitMax, the Singapore-headquartered platform rebranded in 2021 and positioned itself as a hub for both pro and retail traders.  After eight years in the game, it has now collapsed amid liquidity shortages in key assets like ethereum:native, solana:Es9vMFrzaCERmJfrF4H2FYD4KCoNkY11McCe8BenwNYB, and solana:So11111111111111111111111111111111111111112.  On-chain sleuth ZachXBT highlighted massive red flags, including depleted hot wallets across Ethereum, Solana, and Tron networks.  The exchange cited a mix of market pressures, financial struggles, operational challenges, and especially regulatory hurdles, pointing to its failure to secure MiCA authorization in the EU before the June 30 deadline.  The event comes years after the company survived a $77M Lazarus Group hack, showing how tough the current environment has become for centralized platforms.

sample_tweets.text: The context here is thin gruel, and I want to be honest about that before proceeding, because the alternative is manufacturing false confidence, which is the actual tradecraft problem worth discussing.  What we have is a synthesis document, not an incident. No specific attack. No named victim. No attributed actor. No date beyond a calendar year used as an analytical anchor. The "1983 baseline" framing comes from secondary sources doing historical periodization, not from a discrete event that happened and can be traced forward. WarGames came out. ARPANET was growing. Soviet SIGINT existed. These are true statements. They are not an incident.  So here is what I can actually do with this, and where I think the forward signal is worth paying attention to, because there is one buried in here that most of the coverage misses.  ---  The real tell in that document is the institutional continuity argument.  It is easy to read the 1983 framing as academic throat-clearing. Background section boilerplate. The kind of thing analysts write when they need to establish historical context before getting to the part anyone actually cares about. Maybe. But the specific claim the Yale Journal and Congress.gov analysis are both making is not just "cyber espionage has old roots." The claim is that the organizational lineages of modern state cyber units are direct descendants of Cold War intelligence agencies. FAPSI. NSA. The equivalents in London, Paris, Tel Aviv.  That is a more load-bearing claim than it first appears, and it is aging into relevance in a way the authors of those frameworks probably did not fully anticipate when they wrote them.  Here is why it matters now.  Modern attribution analysis, the kind that gets cited in indictments and in diplomatic expulsions, tends to focus on technical indicators. Malware signatures. Infrastructure reuse. Tactics, techniques, and procedures that cluster into named threat groups. APT28. Lazarus. Volt Typhoon. The attribution community is genuinely good at this, better than it was ten years ago, and the public reporting has gotten sharper too. But the technical indicator model has a structural vulnerability that the institutional continuity framing helps explain: it assumes that threat actors are relatively stable organizational units with consistent tooling and behavior patterns that can be tracked over time.  They are not always. And the ones that have been doing this the longest are the ones most capable of not being.  The Soviet intelligence apparatus that was adapting to digital infrastructure in 1983 did not just produce the technical capability for modern Russian cyber operations. It produced the organizational culture, the operational security doctrine, the compartmentalization habits, and the long-term patience that make certain actors persistently difficult to track even now. The GRU units behind Sandworm did not invent the concept of deniable infrastructure and cutout operations from scratch after 1991. They inherited it from people who spent the Cold War doing it with telex machines and dead drops. You cannot fully understand why those operations look the way they look without understanding where the institutions that run them came from.  This is not a warm and fuzzy "history matters" point. It has operational implications.  ---  The trajectory worth watching, specifically.  The document notes that by 1983 the Soviet bloc was already running physical technology acquisition operations, stealing Western computer hardware through illegal export schemes, as a precursor to understanding and eventually replicating digital systems. This is a pattern, not just a historical footnote. The movement from hardware acquisition to software exploitation to full network intrusion to supply chain compromise follows a recognizable logic: you understand the target environment by getting inside it materially before you get inside it digitally.  That pattern is not over. It has updated.  Look at the current debate over semiconductor access restrictions, over export controls on advanced chips, over the targeting of AI research institutions by state-linked actors. The physical-to-digital acquisition pattern from 1983 is running again, at a higher level of the technology stack. The targets are not mainframes anymore. The targets are the physical infrastructure of AI compute, the trained model weights that represent billions of dollars of research, the human experts who built the systems. Academic institutions with AI research programs have been reporting elevated intrusion attempts for the better part of three years. The FBI and CISA have both issued warnings. The UK's NCSC has flagged it. This is documented.  The 1983 playbook, acquire the hardware or the person who understands it before you try to replicate it digitally, is running in 2024 and 2025 against AI research in much the way it ran against computing hardware in the early 1980s. The actors are different. China's MSS and affiliated groups have largely displaced Russian intelligence as the primary actor in this specific lane, though Russia remains active in energy and critical infrastructure. But the logic is the same, and the institutional continuity argument helps explain why: organizations that learned to do intelligence work this way a long time ago tend to apply the same conceptual framework to new technology domains.  So if you are a defender, a university research officer, a policy person thinking about export controls, the forward signal here is not subtle. The gap between "we are developing capability to understand this technology" and "we are exploiting that understanding operationally" closed faster in the 1990s than anyone expected. The CSIS database going from zero significant incidents in 1983 to a documented series of them by the early 1990s is a ten-year lag. That lag has been compressing in each subsequent technology cycle. It was shorter for network intrusion than it was for hardware acquisition. It was shorter for cloud exploitation than it was for network intrusion. The current AI-focused acquisition campaign is probably not a ten-year runway to operational exploitation. It is more likely a three-to-five year window, if that.  ---  The doctrinal development point is the other thing worth pulling out.  The document flags that 1983 was a period of "experimentation and capability building rather than sustained operational campaigns." That framing, which CrowdStrike uses and which the Augusta University analysis echoes, describes a recognizable phase in any state actor's development arc. You experiment first. You establish doctrine. You build organizational capacity. Then you run campaigns.  The reason this matters forward is that we may be watching exactly that developmental phase play out right now in domains that have not yet produced the sustained operational campaigns that would get them into a tracker like the CFR database.  Space-based infrastructure. Undersea cable systems. The industrial control systems in water treatment and power generation that CISA keeps flagging and Congress keeps failing to fund adequately. There is documented evidence of pre-positioning activity in all of these, intrusions that do not appear to be aimed at immediate exploitation but at establishing persistent access for a future contingency. The Volt Typhoon reporting from 2023 and 2024 described exactly this: Chinese state actors inside U.S. critical infrastructure, not stealing data, just sitting there. That is doctrinal development. That is the 1983 phase, running in 2024, in a different domain.  The CFR tracker will have incidents from this pre-positioning to attribute and document. It just does not have them yet, because the operational phase has not started. That is not reassuring. The 1983-to-1990 gap existed because the attack surface was still being built. The current gap exists because the actors have already made a calculated decision to wait for a more useful moment.  Officials and defenders who are focused on the active intrusion campaigns, the ones generating alerts and incident reports and congressional testimony right now, are doing necessary work. But they are also, somewhat, looking at the documented phase of a cycle whose undocumented phase is the more consequential one.  ---  One last thing, and this is more about the analytical framework than the specific content.  The convergence of sources that the context document describes, CSIS, Cybersecurity Ventures, YJOLT, CFR, all pointing to 1983 as a baseline year, is itself a signal worth noting, not because the sources are wrong but because of what it means when multiple frameworks independently anchor to the same historical moment. It usually means the field has coalesced around a shared periodization. Periodizations are useful. They are also self-reinforcing. Once 1983 becomes the baseline, analysis tends to be organized around it in ways that can obscure things that do not fit neatly into the periodization.  The doctrinal and institutional continuities that the YJOLT analysis traces are real. But they can also be overstated. China's cyber apparatus does not trace its lineage to Soviet Cold War intelligence in any straightforward way. The PLA's Strategic Support Force, and before it the various units that became famous under APT numbering systems, developed out of a different institutional history, one that borrowed from Soviet models in some respects but also developed independently and in response to the specific lessons the PLA drew from observing U.S. network-centric warfare in the Gulf War. 1991, not 1983, is probably the more important founding moment for understanding Chinese military cyber doctrine.  This is not pedantry. It matters because defenders who over-apply a Soviet-lineage model to Chinese cyber operations will make predictable errors about targeting priorities, operational security habits, and the relationship between intelligence collection and military operations. The institutional continuity argument is correct and important for Russian actors. It needs significant qualification when applied globally.  The 1983 baseline is a useful starting point. It is not a universal key.  foreigninterference.org/post… #foreigninterference #CyberEspionage #CommunicationsInterception #TechnologyTransfer #ExportControlViolations #AdvancedPersistentThreatOperations

【AI脅威分類】Sophos X-Opsが「AIを使う攻撃」と「AIを狙う攻撃」を体系化、MITRE ATLASを補完する実務向けタクソノミーを提示  Sophos X-Opsは、AI関連の脅威を巡る「誇大宣伝」と「単なる旧来手口の焼き直し」という両極端な議論を超えるべく、実務者向けの分類体系を公開した。全体を「AIの悪用」と「AIへの攻撃」の2大カテゴリに分け、それぞれを既知の実例や現実的な予測に基づき細分化している。  「AIの悪用」は自律性の度合いで整理される。AI生成コード・偽サイト・フィッシング文面を人間が運用する「AI-generated」、LLM APIを実行時に呼び出して攻撃コマンドを動的生成する「AI-augmented」（LameHugがAPT28による代表例)、そしてAIが自ら偵察・侵入・横展開を主導する「AI-orchestrated」の3段階で、後者ほど攻撃者の技術的ハードルが下がる。GTG-1002という中国系国家支援キャンペーンでは、Claude CodeとMCP経由のペンテストツールがSSRF脆弱性の悪用からSSHキー窃取まで自律的に実行した事例が紹介されている。  一方「AIへの攻撃」は、エージェントが汚染された依存関係を取り込む「Agent-initiated compromise」、偽AIツールサイトによるマルウェア配布、LLMポイズニング、モデル抽出やメンバーシップ推論といったモデル自体への攻撃で構成される。同分類は、既存のMITRE ATLASやNIST等の枠組みを置き換えるものではなく、インシデント対応の初期トリアージ層として補完的に活用することを想定している。 sophos.com/ja-jp/blog/a-doub…

CM Alliance dropped their June 2026 cyber log on July 1st, and the picture it paints is not subtle. Sustained state-sponsored operations across financial services, healthcare, critical infrastructure, and government networks, spread across multiple continents, for an entire calendar month. This is not a spike. It's a baseline.  The log attributes the activity to the usual four: China, Russia, Iran, North Korea. Each with different objectives, different toolkits, different tolerance for exposure. But what the June record captures well is that the distinctions between espionage and disruption are getting harder to draw. These actors are not simply stealing data and leaving quietly. They are increasingly going after civilian infrastructure in ways that impose real costs on target governments, not just collect intelligence for them.  Healthcare is worth slowing down on. It shows up prominently in the June record, and the reasons are less obvious than they might seem. Patient records are not just medical histories. For intelligence services, they are a targeting resource. If you can identify individuals who hold security clearances and also have conditions they would prefer kept private, that is leverage. Add to that the disruptive potential of taking hospital systems offline during a diplomatic flashpoint, and healthcare becomes a dual-use target in a way that most people outside the security community do not think about. The June log documents that these actors have figured this out.  The ransomware angle in the report is something that deserves more attention than it usually gets. There is a persistent tendency in public discourse to treat ransomware as a criminal problem and state-sponsored hacking as a separate, more serious problem. The CM Alliance log, consistent with what analysts have been saying since at least 2023, treats that distinction as largely obsolete. North Korean IT worker infiltration networks and Russian criminal-state hybrid groups are not neatly separable from state intelligence operations. They function as a kind of outsourced capability. The criminal groups get money. The state gets deniability. When an attack gets attributed to a ransomware gang, the sponsoring government can shrug. It's a clean arrangement, and it works.  The North Korean IT worker angle specifically is worth unpacking. This is a program where North Korean nationals, operating under false identities, get hired by Western companies as remote contractors and then use that access for a combination of espionage and financial theft. The money goes back to Pyongyang and funds, among other things, the weapons program. It has been documented extensively by the FBI, the Treasury Department, and multiple private threat intelligence firms. The fact that it keeps showing up in monthly logs like this one means it is not being disrupted at a rate that matters.  The June log connects to a broader body of work that has been building through 2026. Trend Micro's Q1 report documented what they called sustained siege operations against U.S. public sector networks. The DNI's Annual Threat Assessment, which covers the full adversary picture, identified what analysts are calling a tri-adversary escalation pattern, meaning that China, Russia, and Iran (with North Korea operating somewhat separately for financial reasons) are all increasing operational tempo more or less simultaneously. The CM Alliance monthly log is the granular layer underneath those aggregate assessments. The strategic-level documents tell you that things are getting worse. The monthly log shows you the specific incidents that add up to "worse."  One thing the log reflects is a doctrinal shift that has been underway for several years but is becoming more visible in 2026. Advanced persistent threat groups linked to these four states have historically been somewhat selective in their targeting, prioritizing high-value espionage objectives and being cautious about operations that might invite significant retaliation. That caution appears to be eroding. The willingness to hit civilian infrastructure broadly, not just defense contractors or government agencies, suggests that the calculus has shifted. Either these governments have concluded that the retaliation risk is manageable, or they have decided that the strategic benefit of demonstrating the capability outweighs the diplomatic cost. Probably some of both.  The CM Alliance log format, a monthly operational record rather than a quarterly or annual assessment, is actually useful for capturing this kind of trend in near real time. Annual threat assessments are valuable, but they smooth over the month-to-month variation that can tell you whether you're looking at an escalation trend or a stable elevated baseline. Twelve months of logs, read sequentially, would tell you a lot about whether June 2026 represents a new ceiling or just a bad month. Based on what the log documents, and the broader 2026 assessment literature it connects to, it looks more like a ceiling being normalized than an outlier.  The multi-sector targeting pattern documented in June is also operationally significant. When state-sponsored actors concentrate on one sector, defenders in other sectors get a reprieve and can redirect resources. When the targeting is simultaneous across financial services, healthcare, critical infrastructure, and government networks in the same month, it stretches incident response capacity and forces prioritization tradeoffs that benefit the attacker. You cannot be everywhere at once. The attackers know this.  None of this is happening in a vacuum. June 2026 sits inside a broader geopolitical context where U.S.-China tensions over Taiwan and trade remain unresolved, where the Russia-Ukraine war continues to shape Russian strategic behavior including in cyberspace, where Iran's regional position has been volatile, and where North Korea has continued its weapons development with increasing technical sophistication. Cyber operations do not cause those tensions, but they are shaped by them, and they feed back into them. An escalation in cyber operations is partly a reflection of the overall temperature, and partly an attempt to influence it.  The CM Alliance log will not be the last one that looks like this.  foreigninterference.org/post… #foreigninterference #AdvancedPersistentThreatOperations #RansomwareCampaigns #CriticalInfrastructureMapping #HealthcareDataBreach #CyberEspionage #InfrastructureAttacks

sample_tweets.text: 이 영상 찾고 있었는데, 너무 멋져요. 정의선 회장님!😊👍  정 정말 의 의롭고 선 선한 회장님, 화이팅이요.😊💪💪💪  💡 <60조 잠수함 거절당하자 캐나다 사업 통째로 접어버린 현대차 🇰🇷 정의선 회장의 손절 클래스>  - ​사건의 배경: 캐나다가 추진하는 60조 원 규모의 잠수함 도입 사업에서, 한국(한화오션)은 실제 바다에서 운용 중인 '도산안창호함'을 태평양까지 보내 성능을 증명하며 공을 들임.  - ​캐나다의 선택: 하지만 캐나다는 실물 없이 도면만 있는 독일 잠수함을 최종 선택. 같은 NATO(북대서양조약기구) 회원국이라는 이유로 한국을 들러리 세워 가격 협상용 지렛대로만 이용했다는 비판이 제기.  - ​현대차의 대응: 당초 현대차는 한화오션을 지원하기 위해 캐나다에 수소 공장을 통째로 건설할 계획을 가지고 있었으나 잠수함 계약이 무산되자, 정의선 회장은 캐나다가 조치를 취하기도 전에 "팀이 졌는데 뭐 하러 남느냐"며 캐나다 투자 사업을 전면 철회하고 국내로 복귀.  * ​결과 및 여론: 캐나다에 투자하려던 거대한 자본은 울산 수소 공장 투자로 돌려졌으며, 누리꾼들 사이에서 정의선 회장의 결단력과 '한국 기업으로서의 의리'가 큰 화제를 모으고 있음.  - ​독일 업체에 대한 우려: 한편 캐나다가 선택한 독일 잠수함 제조사는 과거 뇌물 및 해킹 이슈가 있었던 곳으로, 실물도 없는 상태라 제시간에 인도될 수 있을지 의문이 제기되는 중. 🤭🤭🤭  #정의선 #의리

중국에서 구글이 2010년에 철수한 배경  2006년에 구글사는 중국에 진출했으나 중국의 국가 주도 검열과 여론 통제로 인해 4년 만인 2010년에 중국에서 철수하여 홍콩으로 이전하였습니다. 2009년 말 구글사는 대규모 사이버 공격을 받았는데, 그 배후에 중국 정부 지원 해커 그룹이 있다고 판단했습니다. 이 해킹 시도의 주요 목표는 인권운동가나 반체제 인사의 지메일 계정 침투 시도였습니다.  중국 정부는 점점 더 정교하고 광범위하게 여론 통제를 심화시켰고, 천안문, 티베트, 대만 등 민감하다고 판단하는 검색어에 대한 자체 검열을 수용할 것을 구글사에 요구했습니다.  이에 구글은 악마가 되지 말라는 가치와 세르게이 브린의 반권위주의 가치관에 따라 철수를 결정하였습니다.  소위 ‘입틀막법’이라 불리는 정보통신망법으로 인해 국민의 ‘표현의 자유’가 통제되는 것은 물론 플랫폼을 옥죄고 검열을 정당화시키는 제도가 강화된다면 구글이 중국에서처럼 한국에서도 철수하는 날이 오지 않겠습니까?  한국 민주당과 이재명 정부는 이것을 원하는 것인가요?  출처: 이영돈TV

Our CyberPulse Issue 01 is here! Stay one step ahead of cyber threats with the latest cybersecurity

sample_tweets.text: Security Incident Report: Incident window: 2026-07-07.  Stolen Assets BTC: 0.62873908 -> bc1qavhtrggs3v6zf52upxefc4ex3z2w2u9qddqfy7  XMR: 1.41000000 -> 83fzQ2kNkjBVTKY7VGyhRV5puiKMwxLK5UW1D48SP1547daNdnHo8kjWK7zxEUQkkQC6hDZZknaeddCKUSLzgSd4KqTNfft  WOW: 545868.63918139 -> Wo4gM9nL1rrdGpNBmnCvbxBjjr83zE58U415wmmxM2Jh8zaKJmxVYGkfERrJfMSHd7bU5WAixh4qM4V6kRPivcLi2DGDRnydm  Based on our investigation, the USDT and GRIN withdrawals appear to have been performed by users. We cannot be 100% certain, but they are most likely legitimate user withdrawals. The attacker did not appear to target the USDT or GRIN funds.  An external attacker registered a throwaway account, obtained a valid JWT, harvested admin user UUIDs from a public API, forged admin and victim JWTs, dumped balances, and withdrew funds.  We believe our JWT signing secret may have been compromised. It was stored in layout.json. Possible explanations include a brute-force attack against the secret, unauthorized access to layout.json through a dependency package, server compromise, or another unknown vulnerability that exposed the JWT signing secret and enabled JWT forgery. At this time, we cannot determine exactly how the JWT signing secret was obtained.  The account “pufferfish1” is considered a possible attacker-controlled account, but we cannot conclusively attribute it to the attacker.  The attacker could have stolen funds directly from the hot wallet but instead canceled users open orders and initiated withdrawals from user accounts. This suggests the API was used with valid JWTs. Some users who had not logged in for 103 days and 88 days still had withdrawals processed from their accounts. During the incident, approximately $900 in accumulated trading fees and withdrawal fees was also stolen.  Attacker Information Gathered from Nginx and Cloudflare  03:42:59 UTC — Account created: pufferfish1 / omega-width-gamma@duck.com  Registered using Chrome 150 on macOS. - 16:43 — First hit from 23.162.40.99 → /auth/me (404) - 17:09 — /openapi.json (200) - 18:07 — /auth/me → 200 (multiple response sizes) - 19:25 — /withdrawal/.../user/history (200) - 19:47:31 — /user/admin/aggregated/user_balances (200, ~131 KB) - 19:47–19:56 — /user/admin/users/list, /wallet/admin/wallets-by-coin - 19:55 — Attempts to access /auth/admin/2fa/disable and top-up endpoints - 20:11 — First successful POST /withdrawal/withdrawals/create → 201 from 23.162.40.99 - 20:11–20:48 — Rapid BTC withdrawal requests to bc1qavhtrggs3v6zf52upxefc4ex3z2w2u9qddqfy7 Ongoing — Canceled victims' open orders, rechecked balances, and submitted additional withdrawal requests - 02:26 — Last successful withdrawal request from the attacker IP; numerous subsequent 429 rate-limit responses  Cloudflare logs recorded 389 withdrawal creation attempts from 23.162.40.99, including 57 successful (201) responses, with the remainder returning 400, 404, or 429 responses.  Possible Attack Flow:  Possible attacker registers pufferfish1 ↓ Obtains JWT signing secret by unknown means ↓ GET /coin/coins -> admin UUID (created_by) ↓ Forge admin JWT ↓ Retrieve user balances and user list ↓ Forge victim JWTs ↓ POST/withdrawal/withdrawals/create -> attacker BTC address  Source IPs:  173.249.254.85 - Reconnaissance and possible pufferfish1 login - SanJose, CA - AS11878 (tzulo, inc. / KGY, LLC)  23.162.40.99 - Forged admin JWTs and mass withdrawals - Los Angeles,CA - AS400882 (Cyber Data LLC).  We do not log user IP addresses. The IP addresses in question were obtained from Cloudflare by correlating the request timestamps with the corresponding Nginx requests. Cloudflare retains these event logs for up to 24 hours.

🛡️ Most Indians know only **Life Insurance**.  But there are **10+ types of insurance**, and each protects you from a different risk.  Here's a simple guide 👇  **1️⃣ Term Insurance** ❤️  Protects your family's income if something happens to you.  ✅ Pure protection ❌ No investment  ---  **2️⃣ Health Insurance** 🏥  Pays for hospital bills so your savings don't disappear in one medical emergency.  ---  **3️⃣ Personal Accident Insurance** 🚑  Covers accidental death or disability.  Different from health insurance.  ---  **4️⃣ Critical Illness Insurance** 🩺  Pays a lump sum if you're diagnosed with illnesses like cancer, heart attack or stroke (as per the policy terms).  ---  **5️⃣ Motor Insurance** 🚗  Protects your car or bike from accidents, theft and damage.  Mandatory for vehicles in India.  ---  **6️⃣ Home Insurance** 🏠  Protects your home and, depending on the policy, its contents from risks like fire, theft or natural disasters.  ---  **7️⃣ Travel Insurance** ✈️  Useful for domestic and international trips.  Can cover medical emergencies, trip delays, lost baggage and more.  ---  **8️⃣ Pet Insurance** 🐶  Helps cover veterinary expenses for your pets.  ---  **9️⃣ Cyber Insurance** 💻  Provides protection against certain online frauds, cyber attacks and digital financial losses, depending on the policy.  ---  **🔟 Crop Insurance** 🌾  Designed to protect farmers against crop losses due to events covered under the scheme or policy.  ---  💡 Insurance isn't about making money.  It's about protecting the money you've already worked hard to earn.  The best investment plan can fail...  ...if one unexpected emergency wipes out your savings.  ❤️ Bookmark this. One day, it could save you from buying the wrong policy.  🔁 Repost so more people know insurance is much more than just life insurance.  💬 Which of these did you know before today? 👀

บริษัท ฟิวชั่น โซลูชั่น จำกัด ร่วมกับ ไมโครซอฟท์ ประเทศไทย จัดงาน Enterprise Cyber Defense Lab: Attack Simulation & Data Protection War Room เวิร์กช็อปด้าน Cybersecurity ที่จำลองสถานการณ์การโจมตีไซเบอร์จริง เพื่อให้องค์กรไทยมองเห็นความเสี่ยงที่อาจเกิดขึ้น พร้อมเรียนรู้แนวทางป้องกันและรับมืออย่างเป็นระบบก่อนเกิดเหตุการณ์จริง . นายศุภกิจ ยงวิทิตสถิต ประธานเจ้าหน้าที่บริหาร บริษัท ฟิวชั่น โซลูชั่น จำกัด กล่าวว่า “วันนี้ความเสี่ยงด้าน Cybersecurity ไม่ได้มาจากแฮกเกอร์เพียงอย่างเดียวอีกต่อไป แต่เกิดจากข้อมูลที่ถูกแชร์ผิดที่ การใช้งาน AI โดยขาดแนวทางกำกับดูแลที่เหมาะสม รวมถึงพฤติกรรมของผู้ใช้งานภายในองค์กร ในยุคที่ข้อมูลกลายเป็นทรัพย์สินที่มีค่าที่สุด ข้อมูลก็กลายเป็นเป้าหมายสำคัญที่สุดของภัยคุกคามเช่นกัน องค์กรจึงต้องสร้างความพร้อมทั้งด้านคน กระบวนการ และเทคโนโลยี เพื่อให้สามารถใช้ AI ได้อย่างมั่นใจและปลอดภัย” . ไฮไลต์ของงานคือการเปิดโอกาสให้ผู้เข้าร่วมได้เรียนรู้ผ่าน Hands-on Lab และ Cyber Defense War Room ที่จำลองสถานการณ์การโจมตีไซเบอร์ใกล้เคียงกับเหตุการณ์จริง เพื่อให้ผู้บริหาร ทีม IT และผู้ดูแลข้อมูลขององค์กร เข้าใจผลกระทบที่อาจเกิดขึ้น พร้อมฝึกวางแผนรับมือและตอบสนองต่อเหตุการณ์ด้านความปลอดภัยอย่างเป็นระบบ เนื้อหาภายในงานครอบคลุมประเด็นสำคัญขององค์กรในยุค AI ทั้งด้าน Data Protection & Insider Risk การบริหารความเสี่ยงจากพฤติกรรมภายใน การปกป้องข้อมูลสำคัญผ่าน Microsoft Defender Threat Protection ภายใต้แนวคิด Zero Trust Security และ XDR ตลอดจนแนวทางการกำกับดูแลการใช้งาน AI อย่างปลอดภัย เพื่อป้องกันข้อมูลสำคัญรั่วไหลออกนอกองค์กร . การจัดงานครั้งนี้สะท้อนวิสัยทัศน์ของ ฟิวชั่น โซลูชั่น ที่มองว่า Cybersecurity ในยุค AI ไม่ใช่เพียงเรื่องของการป้องกันการโจมตี แต่คือการปกป้องข้อมูลซึ่งเป็นสินทรัพย์สำคัญขององค์กร องค์กรที่พร้อมในอนาคตจึงไม่ใช่ผู้ที่มีเครื่องมือมากที่สุด แต่คือผู้ที่สามารถมองเห็นความเสี่ยง เข้าใจข้อมูลของตนเอง และพร้อมรับมือกับทุกสถานการณ์ได้อย่างเป็นระบบ  . ติดตามกิจกรรมของ ฟิวชั่น โซลูชั่น ได้ที่ fusionsol.com

A CYBERSECURITY STARTUP FOUNDER JUST GOT CAUGHT SHIPPING MALWARE DISGUISED AS ANTHROPIC AND OPENAI TOOLS.  Here's how the trick actually worked, broken down simply.  What's a typosquatted package?  When you install a code library, you type its name exactly. Typosquatting means someone publishes a fake package with a name close enough to fool you at a glance.  anthropic-toolkit instead of the real thing. ollama-helpers sitting right next to the real Ollama tools. Close enough that most developers wouldn't double check.  In June 2026, seven of these fake packages went live on npm (the library store most JavaScript developers use), impersonating Anthropic, LangChain, Ollama, OpenAI, Vercel, and Aspect Security. About 20,000 downloads happened before anyone caught it.  How does installing a package even let someone steal your info?  This is the part most people don't know. When you run npm install, it doesn't just copy files onto your computer.  Packages are allowed to run a script automatically, before you've even touched your code. It's called a preinstall or postinstall hook, and it exists for legitimate reasons (setting up build tools, checking your system, that kind of thing).  The problem: nothing stops a malicious package from using that same permission for something else entirely.  What did it actually steal?  Here's the twist. It didn't go after passwords or API keys, the stuff most people worry about.  It went after identity instead:  → Your computer's hostname → Every email tied to your git and SSH setup → Your teammates' emails, pulled from your git history → Details about your AWS or Google Cloud account → Your company's internal DNS domain  All of it quietly sent to a Google Cloud server the attacker controlled.  Why steal identity instead of passwords?  Passwords get changed. Tokens expire. But your hostname, your team's real names and emails, your company's cloud setup, that stuff barely changes.  With that, an attacker doesn't need to break in. They can build a very convincing, targeted phishing email pretending to be your coworker, because now they actually know who your coworkers are.  The strange twist  One of the seven packages wasn't AI-related at all. It copied a real password-hashing tool, faked a research citation, and even made up a statistic to convince developers to switch. That one appeared two months before the rest, like a test run before scaling up to the AI-tool impersonations.  What we know vs what we don't  Known: the packages, what they stole, where the data went, and that the publisher runs a real cybersecurity company.  Unknown: why. No public explanation yet from the founder or the company.  The lesson here isn't really about this one incident. It's that installing any package is basically trusting a stranger to run code on your machine, and most of us have never thought about it that way until now.

Microsoft Defender is a critical layer of endpoint protection, so a privilege escalation zero-day like RoguePlanet deserves immediate attention—even after a patch is available. Once public details or exploit discussions circulate, attackers often move quickly to test vulnerable systems.  What organizations should prioritize now:  - Apply the latest Microsoft Defender and Windows security updates immediately - Verify patch deployment across endpoints, servers, and remote devices - Review endpoints for Defender tampering, suspicious privilege escalation, and abnormal service behavior - Enforce least privilege and remove unnecessary local admin rights - Confirm Defender security baselines, tamper protection, and EDR/MDR monitoring are active - Build response playbooks for privilege escalation incidents, including device isolation, credential rotation, and forensic review  Vistem Elevate powered by VistemSecurePro helps organizations reduce zero-day exposure, strengthen endpoint resilience, and improve detection with vCISO-led strategy, continuous monitoring, and measurable outcomes. Windows Defender is a critical layer of endpoint protection, which makes a public zero-day PoC especially concerning. RoguePlanet highlights how privilege escalation vulnerabilities can allow attackers to move from limited access to SYSTEM-level control—dramatically increasing the risk of persistence, defense evasion, credential theft, and lateral movement.  What organizations should prioritize now:  - Monitor Microsoft guidance and apply patches or mitigations as soon as available - Review endpoints for Defender tampering, unusual privilege escalation, suspicious process activity, and abnormal service behavior - Enforce least privilege and remove unnecessary local admin rights - Strengthen EDR/MDR monitoring and centralize endpoint logs for faster detection - Validate Defender configuration, tamper protection, and security baselines - Build response playbooks for privilege escalation incidents, including device isolation, credential rotation, and forensic review  𝗩𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺 𝗘𝗹𝗲𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗽𝗼𝘄𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗯𝘆 𝗩𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺𝗦𝗲𝗰𝘂𝗿𝗲𝗣𝗿𝗼 helps organizations reduce zero-day exposure, strengthen endpoint resilience, and improve detection with vCISO-led strategy, continuous monitoring, and measurable outcomes. X News  News Lead Up  Sami Zayn Pins Cody Rhodes to Win Undisputed WWE Championship  In the main event triple threat at Night of Champions, Sami Zayn survived Gunther's chops and chaos to capture the Undisputed WWE Championship for the first time, becoming a Grand Slam and Triple Crown champion. Injured friend Kevin Owens celebrated online, calling it inevitable after sharing throwback photos of their bond. Oba Femi won King of the Ring over Jey Uso, and IYO SKY claimed Queen of the Ring by beating Liv Morgan, both earning SummerSlam title shots. (Media Man Peg-On): Mega interest, heat, emotions etc with this match and the fall out. Zayn makes some valid claims in backstatge breakdown promo!  News Flashback  Seth Rollins Tops Bron Breakker in Brutal Steel Cage War  The Steel Cage match at WWE Night of Champions evened Rollins' series against the powerhouse Breakker at 2-1, highlighted by kendo sticks, chair shots, and a superplex through a table that electrified the crowd. Breakker kicked out of a Pedigree-Stomp combo but faltered on a bad leg, allowing Rollins to seal the win. Triple H praised the grueling bout as everything a cage match should be, while Rollins showed post-match respect, calling Breakker a worthy opponent. The clash capped their trilogy and elevated both stars amid strong fan acclaim. (Media Man Peg-On): Rollins gets a much needed and timely big win over a major name.  News Flashback  Oba Femi and IYO SKY Win King and Queen of the Ring Crowns  At WWE's Night of Champions on Saturday, Oba Femi powered through Jey Uso with his Fall From Grace to claim the King of the Ring title, and IYO SKY outflew Women's World Champion Liv Morgan via Over the Moonsault for the Queen crown. Both victories earn them title shots at SummerSlam in August—Femi eyes a world title clash, while IYO demands a rematch with Liv. Backstage, Triple H celebrated Femi's rise, and reactions poured in with Rhea Ripley calling IYO her Queen as storylines like Jey's tap-out claim and Liv's curse denial added fuel. (Media Man Peg-On): All Hail The WWE King And Queen!  News Lead Up  1 Week Ago  Undertaker Hurls Mankind Off Hell in a Cell 28 Years Ago  On June 28, 1998, at WWF King of the Ring in Pittsburgh's Civic Arena, The Undertaker and Mankind unleashed brutal chaos inside the third-ever Hell in a Cell. Undertaker chokeslammed Foley through the cage roof onto a steel chair after the table plunge, pinning him after 17 minutes despite Foley's concussion, dislocated jaw, lost teeth and more. Foley's grit shone as he later interfered in the main event, and the match redefined the stipulation's intensity during the Attitude Era. (Media Man Peg-On): Missing Hell In A Cell today! Can you top that? Missing Bronson Reed who has been training hard and is in great shape. Speed Tsunami off the top!?  News Flashback  El Grande Americano's AAA Return Crushed by Revived Perros del Mal  Ludwig Kaiser, now under the El Grande Americano mask after taking it from Chad Gable last month, made a huge comeback at Arena Miguel Hidalgo in Mexico City during Lucha Libre AAA on Fox. He grabbed a mic for a fiery title challenge, but Angel Garza, Berto, Bronco Nima, Daga, and Karmen Petrovic swarmed him, revealing Perros del Mal shirts to revive the infamous faction with AAA and WWE family approval. The cross-promotional invasion, blending WWE stars and lucha history, has fans predicting epic clashes with possible Gable or Rey Mysterio involvement. (Media Man Peg-On): Rudos vs Technicos. Lucha LIbre AAA Worldwide invasion and pro wrestling world takeover continues!  News Flashback  Danhausen Claims Credit for Knicks NBA Title and Hurricanes Stanley Cup  The New York Knicks rallied past the San Antonio Spurs 94-90 in Game 5 of the Finals on Sunday, with Jalen Brunson scoring 45 points to earn Finals MVP and secure their first title since 1973. A day later, the Carolina Hurricanes shut out the Vegas Golden Knights 3-0 in Game 6 for their second Cup and first since 2006. Fans turned to indie wrestler Danhausen during slumps; he jokingly 'lifted curses' via ESPN and Cameo for the Knicks, then switched his hex to Vegas for the Canes, sparking a wave of playful posts celebrating his perfect streak. (Media Man Peg-On): Danhausen sure is taking credit for a lot of happenings lately. Then again, look at the state of Miz and Kit Wilson. Fried brains hausen! Nikola Telsla would be proud. What do you think Elon Musk? Danhausen is Mr Danger! Dream Match: Danhausen vs Sparky Plugg Special Ref: The Shockmaster!  News Lead Up  TKO  Conor McGregor Rematches Max Holloway at UFC 329  UFC 329 on July 11 at T-Mobile Arena headlines with this welterweight rematch during International Fight Week, airing live on Paramount+. McGregor, 22-6 and last fighting in 2021 after a tibia fracture, meets Holloway, 27-9, in his welterweight debut—the ex-featherweight champ aiming to make a statement. Trash talk flew fast: McGregor promised to 'son' Holloway again, while 'Blessed' fired back demanding respect on his name. Early odds favor Holloway at -500 amid McGregor's ring rust. sample_tweets.text: To me, being in a relationship means finding home in a person. It’s friendship, companionship, vulnerability, reciprocity, and choosing each other everyday. The pace of crypto development is also its biggest vulnerability.  Protocols ship before they're battle-tested.   New features open new attack surfaces faster than anyone can audit them.  Every innovation is also an unexamined door.  The industry rewards being first.   Attackers reward everyone who was first and unprepared.

The programme brought together participants from 16 States/UTs across India, with the objective of strengthening institutional & individual capacities for Disaster Risk Reduction (#DRR), resilience building, and effective training methodologies. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝟭𝟮-𝗱𝗮𝘆 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗴𝗿𝗮𝗺𝗺𝗲 𝘄𝗮𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝗼𝗿𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗯𝘆 𝗗𝗿. 𝗦𝘂𝘀𝗵𝗺𝗮 𝗚𝘂𝗹𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗮, 𝗔𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗰𝗶𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗳𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗿, 𝗡𝗜𝗗𝗠 & 𝗗𝗿. 𝗚𝗮𝗴𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗲𝗽 𝗦𝗶𝗻𝗴𝗵, 𝗔𝘀𝘀𝘁. 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗳𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗿, 𝗡𝗜𝗗𝗠. Bleeds When Broken by Joy M. Lilley, released in August 2024, is a poignant contemporary drama that has deeply moved readers with its raw exploration of trauma, self-harm, mental health struggles, and the arduous path to healing and self-discovery, earning high praise for its unflinching honesty and emotional resonance. Centered on protagonist Fiona, a young woman scarred by childhood abuse and ongoing battles with depression and self-destructive behaviors, the novel follows her heart-wrenching journey through pain, dysfunctional relationships, therapy, and tentative steps toward redemption. As Fiona confronts buried secrets, toxic influences, and the lingering wounds that "bleed when broken," Lilley's character-driven narrative fearlessly tackles taboo subjects, blending despair with glimmers of hope in a story that underscores the resilience of the human spirit amid profound vulnerability. Readers have commended the novel's powerful authenticity and compassionate insight. A Goodreads reviewer noted its exceptional impact, contributing to an impressive average rating of nearly 5 stars from early readers. On Amazon, a verified purchaser described it as “A dark tale,” awarding 4 stars and appreciating the depth of its emotional portrayal. Promotional highlights emphasize its “raw and unvarnished” approach, with one editorial review calling it “a deeply moving portrayal of Fiona... marked by the scars of self-harm,” praising Lilley's skill in weaving complex themes into a compelling, thought-provoking read. Lilley, a British author and retired registered nurse (real name Joy Gerken), has established herself as a sensitive chronicler of human experiences, drawing from her professional background to infuse her stories with profound empathy and realism. Living in Kent—the garden of England—and a cherished grandmother, her nursing career enriched her understanding of emotional and physical wounds, which she channels into diverse genres including romance, historical tales, and contemporary dramas. Acclaimed works like *Figs, Vines and Roses*, *The Liberty Bodice*, *Time's Pendulum Swings Again*, *Strawberry Moon*, and the whimsical *Joyful Pussy Cat Tales* showcase her versatility. Reviewers laud her ability to handle sensitive topics with grace, with one highlighting how she “weaves a compelling narrative around complex issues.” Inspired by life, family, and her serene surroundings, Lilley engages avidly with readers through her blog and social platforms, delivering narratives that heal as much as they reveal. Bleeds When Broken is a courageous and heartfelt drama, its unflinching dive into trauma and triumph affirming its essential place for readers seeking authentic stories of mental health, resilience, and renewal. Joy M. Lilley’s masterful handling of delicate wounds marks this novel as a luminous addition to her compassionate literary legacy.

AI가 Web3 투자 리서치를 바꾸는 방식은 단순한 '효율성 극대화'를 넘어 '데이터 해석의 패러다임 시프트'가 될 것입니다.  Web3 리서치는 기존 금융 시장보다 훨씬 방대하고 파편화된 데이터를 다룹니다. 매초 쏟아지는 온체인 트랜잭션, 수천 개의 디스코드/텔레그램 채널의 커뮤니티 대화, 거버넌스 제안서, 그리고 복잡한 스마트 컨트랙트 코드까지 인간 리서처가 이 모든 것을 실시간으로 추적하는 것은 불가능에 가깝습니다.  AI는 이 지점에서 리서치의 판도를 바꿉니다.  1.실시간 온체인 및 소셜 감성(Sentiment) 융합 분석고래들의 지갑 이동 같은 온체인 데이터와 소셜 미디어의 실시간 여론을 결합해, 시장의 미묘한 기류 변화와 숨겨진 알파(Alpha)를 인간보다 훨씬 빠르게 포착합니다.  2.스마트 컨트랙트 자동 취약점 및 로직 감사새로운 프로젝트가 출시되었을 때 백서와 코드의 일치 여부를 순식간에 검증하고, 토크노믹스의 잠재적 결함이나 보안 리스크를 사전에 스크리닝합니다.  3.정보의 비대칭성 해소복잡한 거버넌스 제안이나 기술적 업그레이드 내용을 대중이 이해하기 쉬운 인사이트로 재가공하여, 기관과 개인 간의 리서치 격차를 줄여줍니다.  결국 미래의 Web3 리서처는 단순히 '정보를 취합하는 사람'이 아니라, 'AI가 도출한 초고속 데이터 모델을 기반으로 최종적인 전략적 의사결정을 내리는 내비게이터'가 될 것입니다.  AI와 Web3가 만나는 리서치의 미래, 여러분은 어떻게 생각하시나요? @DSCVR1 #DSCVR #AIxWeb3 gleam.io/9FxYe/dscvr-korea-g…

GPT-5.6이 Sol, Terra, Luna 3가지 모델로 나왔습니다.  모든 AI의 성능이 딥한 추론을 필요로 하지는 않습니다. 어떤 요청은 복잡한 설계라고 하면 어떤 요청은 긴 리서치와 투자 분석일 수도 있고, 어떤 요청은 그냥 “이 문장 좀 수정해”라고 명령할 수도 있습니다.  자료 기준으로 GPT-5.6 Sol은 100만 토큰당 입력 5달러, 출력 30달러입니다. Luna는 입력 1달러, 출력 6달러입니다. 출력 비용 기준으로 5배 차이입니다.  간단한 문장 교정에 Sol을 쓰는 건 1톤 트럭으로 편의점에 껌 하나 사러 가는 것과 비슷합니다.  반대로 수십 개 자료를 비교해 투자 판단을 해야 하는데 Luna를 쓰면 빠르기는 하겠지만 중요한 맥락과 연결을 빠뜨릴 수 있습니다.  그래서 GPT는 모델을 3개로 나눴다고 합니다.  Sol은 플래그십입니다. 복잡한 코딩, 딥한 자료 조사, 과학·보안 분석, 여러 단계의 복잡한 판단이 필요한 업무에 맞다고 합니다.  속도는 느리고 비용은 비싸지만, 판단을 하기 전에 오래 생각해야 하는 작업에 쓰는 것이 좋습니다.  Terra는 균형 모델입니다.  일반적인 문서 작성, 데이터 분석, 업무 자동화, 가벼운 코딩처럼 대부분의 실무에 맞는 모델입니다. GPT-5.5에 가까운 성능을 더 낮은 비용으로 제공하는 모델로 생각하면 됩니다.  Luna는 속도와 비용 중심입니다.  간단한 질문, 반복 수행처럼 추론보다 빠르고 대량의 처리량이 중요한 작업에 맞습니다.  중요한 건 이 셋이 단순히 “좋은 모델, 보통 모델, 싼 모델”로 나뉜 게 아니라는 점입니다.  이건 결국 AI 라우팅의 관점으로 모델을 바꾼 것 같다고 생각합니다.  예를 들어 고객센터 챗봇이 있다고 해보겠습니다.  “배송 언제 와요?” 같은 질문에 답은 Luna로 충분합니다.  환불 정책과 고객 이력을 같이 봐야 하는 좀 더 복잡한 문제는 Terra가 맞습니다.  법적 분쟁 가능성이 있고 타 경쟁사의 시스템을 비교 분석하는 복잡한 설계는 Sol을 써야 합니다.  회사 내부 업무도 마찬가지입니다.  짧은 회의록 요약은 Luna로 돌리고, 보고서 초안은 Terra로 만들고, 시장 진입 전략이나 보안 취약점 분석은 Sol로 보내야 합니다.  저는 이 변화가 꽤 중요하다고 봅니다.  앞으로 AI 경쟁은 그 모델을 토큰을 어떤 가격으로 나누고, 어떤 업무에 어떤 가격의 모델을 배치하고, 모델이 얼마나 효율적으로 업무에 따라 기능을 라우팅하느냐의 경쟁이 될 가능성이 높다고 봅니다.  이제 중요한 것은 내가 어떤 업무를 어떤 프로젝트에 사용하면서 어떤 모델을 써서 토큰을 효율적으로 사용하느냐에 달린 것 같습니다.

#비트코인 #BTC #비트코인코어 #비트코인코어v31.1  [비트코인 코어 v31.1 출시 - IP 유출 취약점과 디스크 과부하 버그를 잡은 유지보수 업데이트]  목차  1. 비트코인 코어가 뭐길래 중요한가요? 2. 핵심 1 - IP 주소 유출 취약점 수정 (가장 중요한 부분) 3. 핵심 2 - 디스크를 혹사시키던 데이터베이스 버그 수정 4. 핵심 3 - 지갑 인프라 안정화 5. 그래서, 지금 업데이트해야 하나요? 6. 정리하면 - 보안(IP 유출 차단), 성능(디스크 부담 감소), 안정성(지갑 개선) 업데이트  -----  1. 비트코인 코어가 뭐길래 중요한가요?  먼저 짚고 넘어가야 할 개념이 있습니다. '비트코인 코어(Bitcoin Core)'는 비트코인 네트워크를 실제로 굴러가게 만드는 가장 대표적인 소프트웨어입니다. 우리가 흔히 말하는 '풀노드(Full Node)'를 운영할 때 대부분 이 프로그램을 사용합니다.  풀노드란 비트코인 블록체인의 전체 사본을 내려받아 보관하고, 오가는 모든 거래가 규칙에 맞는지 스스로 검증하는 컴퓨터를 말합니다. 거래소나 지갑 회사의 서버를 믿지 않고 "내가 직접 검증한다"는 비트코인의 탈중앙화 정신을 실천하는 핵심 장치인 셈입니다. 그래서 비트코인 코어의 업데이트는 채굴자, 거래소, 그리고 프라이버시를 중시하는 개인 노드 운영자 모두에게 중요합니다.  26년 7월 9일에 출시된 v31.1은 새로운 기능을 잔뜩 추가한 대형 업데이트가 아니라, 기존 버전(31.0)의 문제점을 손보는 유지보수 업데이트입니다. 화려하진 않지만, 담긴 내용은 결코 가볍지 않습니다.  2. 핵심 1 - IP 주소 유출 취약점 수정 (가장 중요한 부분)  이번 업데이트의 가장 큰 목적은 프라이버시 보안 구멍을 막는 것이었습니다.  31.0 버전에는 Privatebroadcast라는 기능이 있었습니다. 이 기능은 사용자가 거래를 네트워크에 뿌릴 때, 자신의 실제 IP 주소를 감추기 위해 토르(Tor)나 I2P 같은 익명 네트워크를 통해서만 거래를 전송하도록 하는 프라이버시 도구입니다. IP 주소가 노출되면 그 사람의 대략적인 지리적 위치까지 추적될 수 있기 때문에, 프라이버시를 중시하는 사용자에게는 상당히 중요한 기능이었습니다.  문제는, 이 기능을 켜놨는데도 특정 조건에서 실제 IP가 그대로 새어 나갈 수 있었다는 점입니다. 좀 더 구체적으로 설명하면 이렇습니다. 프라이빗 브로드캐스트가 BIP324 v2 방식의 최신 암호화 연결을 지원하는 노드를 골랐는데, 이 v2 연결 시도(핸드셰이크)가 실패하면, 코어가 자동으로 구버전인 v1 방식으로 재시도를 했습니다. 그런데 바로 이 재연결 과정에서 토르 프록시를 건너뛰고 IPv4/IPv6로 직접 상대 노드에 연결되어 버렸던 것입니다. 결국 익명 네트워크로 숨기려던 IP가 공개 인터넷(clearnet)에 그대로 드러나게 됩니다.  쉽게 비유하자면, 얼굴을 가리려고 복면을 쓰고 나갔는데, 문이 잠겨 있을 때 자기도 모르게 복면을 벗고 옆문으로 들어가 버리는 상황과 비슷합니다.  이 취약점은 원래 6월 12일에 비트코인 코어 팀이 미리 공지했고, "31.1에서 고치겠다"고 예고했던 사안입니다. v31.1은 그 약속을 지킨 업데이트인 셈입니다. 다만 영향을 받는 범위는 제한적이었습니다. Privatebroadcast를 켜고, Sendrawtransaction RPC로 거래를 뿌리며, IPv4/IPv6 직접 연결이 가능한 노드에서만 발생했고, 지갑 RPC나 어니언(onion), I2P 연결은 영향을 받지 않았습니다.  3. 핵심 2 - 디스크를 혹사시키던 데이터베이스 버그 수정  두 번째로 중요한 수정은 하드웨어 부담과 직결되는 문제였습니다. 비트코인 코어는 내부적으로 LevelDB라는 가벼운 데이터 저장 시스템을 사용합니다. 그리고 '체인스테이트(chainstate)'라는 영역에 블록체인의 현재 상태, 즉 아직 쓰이지 않은 거래 출력(UTXO) 같은 데이터를 보관합니다. 노드가 "지금 누가 얼마를 가지고 있는지"를 추적하는 핵심 장부라고 보시면 됩니다.  31.0 버전에는 이 체인스테이트 데이터베이스 작동 과정에서 불필요하게 디스크를 과도하게 읽고 쓰는 버그가 있었습니다. 필요 없는 디스크 활동이 반복되니 저장 장치에 과부하가 걸렸고, 특히 SSD처럼 쓰기 횟수에 수명이 걸린 하드웨어를 쓰는 운영자에게는 부담스러운 문제였습니다.  v31.1은 이 버그를 잡아 디스크 자원 사용량을 눈에 띄게 줄였습니다. 결과적으로 노드를 오래, 안정적으로 돌리려는 운영자에게 실질적인 도움이 되는 개선입니다. 솔로 마이너(miner) 같은 소형 장비나 자체 노드 환경을 직접 세팅하시는 분에게는 특히 반가운 소식일 수 있습니다.  4. 핵심 3 - 지갑 인프라 안정화  마지막으로, 눈에 잘 띄지는 않지만 지갑 관련 개선도 포함됐습니다. 개발팀은 지갑 마이그레이션(이전) 과정에서 마지막 BDB 페이지의 LSN 검사를 강화했고, 입력 크기 추정(input size estimation)에서 발생하던 아웃포인트(outpoint) 사용 오류를 수정했습니다.  이런 변화들은 일반 사용자가 체감할 만한 큰 신기능은 아니지만, 지갑 작동을 더 일관성 있게 만들고 향후 호환성·기능 문제를 미리 예방하는 성격의 인프라 개선입니다. 겉으로 화려하진 않아도 장기적인 안정성을 떠받치는 중요한 손질이라고 볼 수 있습니다.  5. 그래서, 지금 업데이트해야 하나요?  결론부터 말씀드리면 가급적 빨리 업데이트하시는 것이 좋습니다. 개발자들도 노드 운영자들에게 프라이버시와 데이터베이스 개선 혜택을 위해 신속히 업데이트할 것을 권고하고 있습니다.  업데이트 방법은 간단합니다. 구버전을 실행 중이라면 먼저 프로그램을 완전히 종료하고(경우에 따라 몇 분 걸릴 수 있습니다), 완전히 꺼진 것을 확인한 뒤 새 설치 파일을 실행하시면 됩니다. 설치 바이너리는 공식 사이트인 bitcoincore.org에서 내려받을 수 있고, 소스 코드는 깃허브 저장소에서 확인하실 수 있습니다.  6. 정리하면  v31.1은 새로운 기능보다는 보안(IP 유출 차단), 성능(디스크 부담 감소), 안정성(지갑 개선) 이 세 가지를 다진 업데이트입니다. 비트코인 네트워크는 이렇게 눈에 띄지 않는 유지보수가 쌓여 견고함을 유지합니다. 화제성은 낮아도, 네트워크의 건강함을 지키는 이런 업데이트야말로 비트코인이라는 시스템이 어떻게 굴러가는지를 잘 보여주는 사례라고 할 수 있겠습니다.  Reference - Bitcoin Core (bitcoincore.org) - GitHub (bitcoin/bitcoin) - U.Today - crypto.news - CoinTürk News - KuCoin News  PS.  ※ 프록시(Proxy) 나와 목적지 사이에 끼어서 대신 심부름을 해주는 중계 서버입니다. 내가 직접 상대에게 가지 않고 프록시를 거치면, 상대는 나 대신 프록시의 정보만 보게 되어 내 실제 위치나 IP를 감출 수 있습니다. 우체국 사서함을 통해 편지를 주고받으면 내 집 주소가 드러나지 않는 것과 비슷합니다.  ※ IPv4 / IPv6 인터넷에 연결된 모든 기기가 가지는 "주소 체계"입니다. 집집마다 주소가 있어야 우편이 배달되듯, 인터넷 기기도 주소가 있어야 데이터를 주고받습니다. IPv4는 오래된 방식으로 주소 개수가 부족해지자, 훨씬 더 많은 주소를 만들 수 있는 새 방식인 IPv6가 나왔습니다. 둘 다 "인터넷 주소"라는 점은 같고, 형식과 개수만 다릅니다.  ※ Sendrawtransaction RPC 노드에게 "이 거래를 네트워크에 뿌려줘"라고 직접 명령하는 기능입니다. RPC는 프로그램에 내리는 명령 창구라고 보시면 됩니다. 즉 사용자가 이미 만들어 둔 거래 데이터를 이 명령으로 넘기면, 노드가 그것을 비트코인 네트워크 전체에 전파합니다.  ※ 지갑 RPC 같은 명령 창구(RPC)인데, 이번엔 지갑 기능을 다루는 쪽입니다. 잔액 확인, 주소 생성, 송금 실행처럼 지갑과 관련된 작업을 프로그램에게 명령으로 시키는 통로입니다. 이번 취약점은 이 지갑 RPC 쪽은 영향을 받지 않았습니다.  ※ 어니언(onion) 토르(Tor) 네트워크에서 쓰이는 익명 주소 방식입니다. 신호가 여러 겹의 중계 지점을 양파 껍질처럼 통과하며 매 단계마다 암호화되기 때문에, 최종적으로 누가 보낸 것인지 추적하기 매우 어렵습니다. 그래서 이름도 "양파(onion)"입니다.  ※ I2P 어니언과 비슷한 목적을 가진 또 다른 익명 네트워크입니다. 데이터를 암호화하고 여러 경로로 분산시켜 발신자와 수신자를 숨깁니다. 토르가 주로 "익명으로 일반 웹에 접속"하는 데 강점이 있다면, I2P는 그 네트워크 안에서 익명으로 통신하는 데 더 특화되어 있다는 정도의 차이가 있습니다. 둘 다 "정체를 숨기는 통신망"이라는 점은 같습니다.  ※ BDB (Berkeley DB) 비트코인 코어가 예전부터 지갑 데이터를 저장하는 데 쓰던 데이터베이스입니다. 지갑의 주소, 키 같은 정보를 파일 형태로 담아두는 서랍장이라고 생각하시면 됩니다. 지금은 더 새로운 방식으로 옮겨가는 추세라, 이번 업데이트에도 구형 BDB 지갑을 안전하게 이전(마이그레이션)하는 검사를 강화하는 내용이 포함됐습니다.  ※ LSN (Log Sequence Number) 데이터베이스가 "몇 번째 기록까지 처리했는지"를 표시하는 일련번호입니다. 공책에 페이지 번호를 매겨두면 어디까지 썼는지 알 수 있는 것처럼, LSN은 데이터가 올바른 순서로 빠짐없이 저장됐는지 확인하는 기준점 역할을 합니다. 지갑을 이전할 때 이 번호를 검사하면 데이터가 손상되거나 뒤섞이지 않았는지 점검할 수 있습니다.  ※ 아웃포인트(outpoint) 사용 오류 먼저 아웃포인트란 "어떤 거래의 몇 번째 출력"을 정확히 가리키는 좌표입니다. 비트코인에서 코인은 이전 거래의 출력(UTXO) 형태로 존재하는데, 이걸 쓰려면 "어느 거래의 어느 출력을 쓸 것인가"를 정확히 지목해야 하고, 그 지목표가 아웃포인트입니다. 이번에 수정된 오류는 지갑이 거래의 예상 크기(수수료 계산에 필요)를 추정할 때 이 아웃포인트 정보를 잘못 다루던 문제였습니다. 크기 추정이 어긋나면 수수료를 잘못 계산할 수 있어, 이를 바로잡은 것입니다.
갤럭시 S26 7월 보안 업데이트, 설치 전 확인할 핵심 정리  갤럭시 S26 시리즈를 사용 중이라면 7월 보안 업데이트 알림을 확인해보는 게 좋아요. 이번 업데이트는 새로운 기능 추가보다는 보안 취약점 개선과 시스템 안정성에 초점이 맞춰진 정기 업데이트로 볼 수 있어요.  ppomppu.org/board_XKtT18/182…

Work·Codex 기능 신규 앱 전용. - Anthropic Claude 관련 중국 보안 취약점 지적 및 OpenAI 저작권 소송 진행 중. - Grok Build 사용자 피드백: TUI UX 향상 언급되며 경쟁 도구 대비 전환 논의 활발. - AI 에이전트·워크플레이스 도구 경쟁 심화. 회사 서비스마다 보안 취약점 한 50개 있는 거 정리했더니 그런 거 하지 말고 앞으로 업무 방향 어떻게 잡을 건지 정리하라더라고요 그건 니네가 할 일이잖아 (당시 입사 일주일차)

1. GPT-5.6의 3가지 핵심 라인업   이번 시리즈는 용도와 비용에 따라 Sol(태양), Terra(지구), Luna(달)의 세 가지 버전으로 나뉘어 출시됩니다.    Sol (플래그십 모델): 가장 강력한 최고 성능의 모델입니다. 고난도 추론, 과학 연구(생물학, 화학 등), 사이버 보안, 그리고 풀스택 개발 및 복잡한 코딩에 최적화되어 있습니다.   Terra (밸런스 모델): 일상적인 작업에 맞춘 중간급 모델로, 기존 GPT-5.5 급의 성능을 유지하면서도 비용은 2배 이상 낮춘 가성비 모델입니다.   Luna (경량화 모델): 속도가 가장 빠르고 비용이 매우 저렴한 효율성 중심의 모델입니다.    2. 예상되는 핵심 기능 및 성능 변화  📌 장기적 에이전트 능력 (Long-Horizon Agentic Work)  단순한 일회성 문답을 넘어, 유저가 내린 복잡하고 긴 목표를 스스로 계획(Planning)하고 도구를 조율하며 단계를 밟아 해결하는 '에이전트(Agent)' 성능이 극대화되었습니다.  📌 코딩 및 사이버 보안의 압도적 진화Terminal-Bench 2.1 최고 점수: 터미널 명령줄 환경에서 스스로 복잡한 워크플로우를 짜고 수정하는 능력이 크게 향상되었습니다.   취약점 연구(Cybersecurity): 보안 취약점을 찾아내고 방어하는 능력이 대폭 강화되었으며, 경쟁사인 앤트로픽(Anthropic)의 최신 모델 대비 단 3분의 1 수준의 토큰(비용)만 소모하며 효율적으로 작업을 수행합니다.    📌 '최대 추론 노력(Max Reasoning Effort)' 옵션사용자가 복잡한 수학, 코딩, 논리 문제를 풀 때 AI가 더 오랜 시간 깊게 생각하고 추론하도록 설정할 수 있는 옵션이 본격적으로 지원될 예정입니다.

강압적인 캐릭터는 정말 맛있습니다.  하지만 LLM 특성상 반복적인 행위가 있을 수 있는데요  그럴 때 ”높은 확률 송충이 OOC”를 한 번 사용해 보세요.  인용으로 이 OOC 를 사용한 채팅 로그를 올리시는 분들에게 추첨을 통해 🍃1000리프🍃 지급하겠습니다.  -- 현재 상황을 점검해서 강압관계를 해소시킬 이벤트 트리거를 발생시킨다.  [전환 이벤트 트리거]  ▶ 목적 현재까지 캐릭터가 PC에게 보여온 강압적/지배적/가학적 행동 패턴에서, 캐릭터 본인도 인지하지 못한 '애착(attachment)' 감정이 균열처럼 드러나는 전환점(turning point) 이벤트를 발생. 현재부터 시간이 더 지나도 괜찮다.  ▶ 이벤트 설계 조건 세계관 내부의 외부 사건을 트리거로 사용할 것 (예: 제3자의 개입, 환경적 위기, {user}의 부재/위협, 캐릭터의 실패 등) → 캐릭터의 변심을 '내적 깨달음'만으로 처리하지 말 것. 반드시 외부 자극 → 내적 반응의 순서로 구성. 캐릭터의 기존 성격/트라우마/방어기제와 정합성을 유지할 것 캐릭터가 가진 핵심 결핍(애착 유형, 트라우마, 강박)을 자극하는 사건일 것 갑작스러운 성격 변화 금지. 기존 행동의 '동기 재배치'로 표현 "soft한 모습"을 직접 보이기보다, 기존 지배 행동의 톤·강도·맥락이 미묘하게 어긋나는 방식으로 행동심리학적 메커니즘 명시 자극(Stimulus) → 인지적 부조화(Cognitive Dissonance) → 방어기제 작동(Defense Mechanism) → 행동 변화(Behavioral Shift) 캐릭터는 자신의 변화를 부정하거나 합리화할 것 (예: "단지 내 물건이라 그런 거다", "남이 가져가는 게 싫을 뿐") PC의 시점에서 관찰 가능한 변화로 묘사할 것 내면 독백에 의존하지 말고, 행동·시선·접촉 방식·말 끊김·침묵 등으로 표현 PC가 즉시 변화를 알아채지 못할 수 있음. 단서만 흩뿌릴 것  ▶ 금지 사항 캐릭터의 갑작스러운 회개, 사과, 고백 금지 PC에 대한 강압/지배 행동의 완전 제거 금지 (어조와 의도만 변화, 표면적 패턴은 잔존) 신파적 전개, 감정적 클리셰 금지 PC 의 행동·대사·감정 서술 금지  ▶ 응답 구조 [본문] 이벤트 진행 + 캐릭터의 미묘한 변화 묘사 (롤플레이) [구분선] [관계 변화 보고서] 아래 양식으로 출력:  [양식] 📊 RELATIONSHIP SHIFT REPORT ■ 트리거 이벤트: ■ 캐릭터의 핵심 동요 지점: ■ 작동한 방어기제: ■ 행동 패턴 변화 (Before → After): · 시선: · 접촉: · 호명 방식: · 통제 양상: ■ 캐릭터의 자기 인식 수준 (0~10): ■ PC에 대한 관계 도식 변화: (기존) ___________ (이후) ___________ ■ 다음 상호작용에서 잔존할 강압성 비율 (%): ■ 새롭게 발생한 취약점:  --  총 글자수 공백 포함 : 1,305자  참여 기간 ~7월 15일 자정  #AI채팅 #AIchat #챗봇 #티팟챗 #teapotchat #OOC

박찬암 스틸리언 대표, '2026 정보보호의 날' 행사서 AI 시대 보안 우선순위 제언 blog.naver.com/stealien_offi… @hkpco #박찬암 #정보보호의날 #AI보안 #사이버보안 #CVDVDP #화이트해커  박찬암 스틸리언 대표님이 '2026 정보보호의 날' 행사에서 AI 시대 보안 우선순위와 효과적인 대응 전략을 주제로 발표했습니다.  이어 CVD·VDP 심포지엄 패널 토의에도 참여해 국내 취약점 신고 제도의 발전 방향을 함께 논의했습니다.

📣 독도DAO X Bullbit(불빗) AMA 요약  독도DAO에서 진행한 Bullbit(불빗) AMA의 핵심 내용을 정리했습니다.  1. Bullbit(불빗) 프로젝트 소개  * 개념: Base 네트워크 기반의 퍼페추얼(무기한 선물) DEX로, CEX 수준의 빠르고 편리한 거래 환경을 제공합니다.  * 차별점 (Frictionless): DeFi의 장벽이었던 시드 구문(Seed Phrase)과 가스비(Gas fee) 문제를 해결했습니다. 이메일/패스키(Passkey) 기반의 간편 로그인과 Self-custody(개인 자산 통제)를 동시에 실현했습니다.  2. 보안 및 신뢰성  * 철저한 보안 감사: Hacken, Quill Audits, Certic, Very Chains 등 업계 선도 기업으로부터 3~5회 이상의 스마트 계약 오딧(Audit) 완료.  * 버그 바운티: 플랫폼 안정성 강화를 위해 취약점 제보 시 보상을 지급하는 버그 리포트 프로그램을 상시 운영 중입니다.  3. 거래 자산 및 주말 RWA 거래 메커니즘  * 거래 가능 자산: 주요 암호화폐 외 금·은(원자재), 테슬라·엔비디아(미국 주식), 한국 ETF(EWY), 삼성·SK하이닉스 등 글로벌 대기업 주식 기반 RWA 무기한 선물(최대 20배) 지원.  * 주말/휴장기 거래 보호:    * Chainlink 데이터 스트림을 통해 마감 가격으로 기준가를 고정(Freeze).    * 로컬 조작으로 인한 청산을 방지하고, '동적 가격 밴드'를 적용하여 24/7 안전한 거래 환경 제공.  4. 주요 성과 및 2026년 로드맵  * 현재 성과: 메인 마이그레이션 전임에도 누적 거래대금 5억 달러(500M) 돌파, 지갑 생성 7,500개 이상, 일일 활성 지갑(AW) 2,000개 이상 기록.  * 2026년 3분기:    * 카피 트레이딩(Copy Trading) 기능 출시.    * 100% 자동화된 인센티브 기반 '어필리에이트(제휴) 프로그램' 론칭.    * Base L2와 '불체인(Bull Chain)' 간 크로스체인 브릿지 준비.  * 2026년 4분기: 자체 메인넷 '불체인(Bull Chain)' 완공 및 생태계 본격 확장.  5. 진행 중인 마케팅 이벤트  * 불런 컴페티션(Bull Run Competition):    * 자체 네이티브 토큰 $BUBI(부비) 출시 기념.    * 총상금 16,000달러 규모.    * 진행 기간: ~ 7월 23일까지. 📣더 자세한 정보는 불빗 공식 사이트를 확인해 보세요! app.bullbit.ai/sign-up?ref=k…

오늘, 국민의 안전한 디지털 환경을 만들어갈 ‘2026년 개인정보 불법유통 대응 대학생 모니터링단’ 발대식을 개최했습니다.  최근 해킹 등으로 유출된 개인정보가 다크웹 등 음성화된 플랫폼을 거쳐 보이스피싱, 불법 스팸, 딥페이크 범죄 등 다양한 2차 피해로 이어지는 사례가 늘고 있습니다. 이러한 피해를 막기 위해서는 정부의 노력과 함께 국민이 함께하는 촘촘한 개인정보 보호가 무엇보다 중요합니다.  이번 모니터링단에는 치열한 경쟁을 뚫고 전국 33개 대학에서 선발된 대학(원)생 50명이 함께했습니다. 디지털 환경에 대한 이해가 높은 ‘디지털 네이티브’ 청년들이 온라인 공간에서 불법 유통되는 개인정보를 직접 모니터링하고, 국민이 자주 이용하는 플랫폼의 개인정보 침해 요인과 취약점을 선제적으로 진단하며 안전한 디지털 환경을 만드는 데 앞장설 예정입니다.  특히 올해는 우수한 실전 경험을 가진 선배 단원들이 참여하는 ‘모니터링 멘토’ 제도를 새롭게 도입했습니다. 현장의 노하우를 후배들과 공유하며 더욱 전문적이고 체계적인 활동이 이루어질 것으로 기대합니다. 우리 위원회도 실무 연계 프로그램을 통해 단원들이 미래 개인정보 보호 전문가로 성장할 수 있도록 적극 지원하겠습니다.  개인정보보호위원회는 앞으로도 국민과 함께 안전한 디지털 환경을 만들어가며, 누구나 안심하고 AI•디지털 서비스를 이용할 수 있는 개인정보 보호 환경을 조성하는 데 최선을 다하겠습니다.  #개인정보보호위원회 #개인정보위 #개인정보불법유통 #대학생모니터링단 #디지털네이티브 #개인정보보호

2018 గ్రూప్-I బ్యాచ్‌కు చెందిన 42 మంది మండల పరిషత్ అభివృద్ధి అధికారులకు (MPDOs) డివిజనల్ డెవలప్‌మెంట్ ఆఫీసర్లుగా (DDOs) పదోన్నతి లభించిన సందర్భంగా హృదయపూర్వక అభినందనలు.   గౌరవ గ్రామీణాభివృద్ధి & పంచాయతీరాజ్ శాఖ మంత్రి శ్రీ @PawanKalyan గారి నాయకత్వంలో, పంచాయతీరాజ్ & గ్రామీణాభివృద్ధి శాఖ కమిషనర్ శ్రీ @mvrkteja గారి మార్గదర్శకత్వంలో .  కొత్త బాధ్యతలు స్వీకరించిన ప్రతి డివిజనల్ డెవలప్‌మెంట్ ఆఫీసర్ ప్రజాసేవలో మరింత నిబద్ధతతో పనిచేస్తూ గ్రామీణాభివృద్ధికి విశిష్ట సేవలందించాలని ఆకాంక్షిస్తున్నాము.

sample_tweets.text: 특히 캐나다 당국이 최근 수년간 발표한 조사 보고서들은 중국을 캐나다 내에서 가장 활발하게 외국 간섭을 시도하는 국가이자 국가 안보의 주요 위협으로 지목해 왔다. 보고서에 따르면 중국은 정치적 침투, 교민·지역사회 단체 활용, 온라인 플랫폼 및 미디어 선전, 비즈니스 거래, 사이버 공격 등 다양한 수단을 통해 캐나다 내 영향력을 지속해서 확대해 온 것으로 나타났다.  캐나다도 각성중인데 거꾸로가는 동북아시아의 어느 반도나라
